subject: ap-chemistry
theme: Energy is conserved in chemical reactions, and the enthalpy change of any reaction can be calculated from calorimetry data, bond energies, or standard enthalpies of formation using Hess's Law.

core_concepts.title: Heat, Temperature, and Calorimetry
core_concepts.detail: Heat ($q$) is energy transferred due to a temperature difference. $q = mc\Delta T$ where $m$ is mass (g), $c$ is specific heat capacity ($J/g \cdot {^\circ C}$), and $\Delta T$ is temperature change. For a reaction: $q_{rxn} = -q_{solution}$ (heat lost by reaction = heat gained by solution). Endothermic reactions absorb heat ($q_{rxn} > 0$); exothermic release heat ($q_{rxn} < 0$).

core_concepts.title: Enthalpy and State Functions
core_concepts.detail: Enthalpy ($H$) is a state function: its value depends only on the current state, not how you got there. At constant pressure, $\Delta H = q_p$. When a reaction is reversed, $\Delta H$ changes sign. When coefficients are multiplied by a factor, $\Delta H$ is multiplied by the same factor.

core_concepts.title: Hess's Law
core_concepts.detail: Because enthalpy is a state function, $\Delta H$ for an overall reaction equals the sum of $\Delta H$ values for any sequence of steps connecting the same initial and final states. To apply Hess's Law: reverse equations (change sign of $\Delta H$) and multiply as needed, then add.

core_concepts.title: Standard Enthalpies of Formation
core_concepts.detail: The standard enthalpy of formation ($\Delta H_f^\circ$) is the enthalpy change for forming 1 mol of a compound from its elements in their standard states at $25^\circ C$ and 1 atm. By definition, $\Delta H_f^\circ = 0$ for any element in its standard state. $\Delta H_{rxn}^\circ = \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\circ(\text{reactants})$.

core_concepts.title: Bond Enthalpies
core_concepts.detail: Approximate $\Delta H_{rxn}$ using average bond energies: $\Delta H \approx \Sigma \text{(bonds broken)} - \Sigma \text{(bonds formed)}$. Bond breaking is endothermic; bond forming is exothermic. This method gives estimates — it uses average bond energies, not exact values for specific molecules.

core_concepts.title: Phase Changes and Heating Curves
core_concepts.detail: Heating curves show temperature vs. heat added. Horizontal plateaus occur during phase changes (melting, boiling) when all added heat goes to overcoming IMFs (potential energy), not raising temperature. $\Delta H_{fus}$ (solid $\rightarrow$ liquid) and $\Delta H_{vap}$ (liquid $\rightarrow$ gas) are always positive (endothermic). Condensation and freezing are exothermic.

formulas.name: Heat transfer (calorimetry)
formulas.description: Calculates heat absorbed or released by mass $m$ (g) with specific heat capacity $c$ ($J/g \cdot {^\circ C}$) undergoing temperature change $\Delta T$ ($^\circ C$ or K).

formulas.name: Standard enthalpy of reaction
formulas.description: Uses tabulated standard enthalpies of formation to calculate the overall enthalpy change; stoichiometric coefficients are multiplied with each $\Delta H_f^\circ$ value.

formulas.name: Bond enthalpy approximation
formulas.description: Estimates reaction enthalpy using average bond dissociation energies $D$; positive sign means energy input (endothermic), negative means energy output (exothermic).
formulas.example: $\text{H}_2 + \text{Cl}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{HCl}$: broken: H–H (436) + Cl–Cl (242) = 678 kJ; formed: 2(H–Cl) = 2(431) = 862 kJ. $\Delta H \approx 678 - 862 = -184$ kJ.

exam_tip: In Hess's Law problems, look for reactions that need to be reversed or multiplied. If the target reaction has a compound on the product side, find a formation reaction for that compound and reverse it if the compound appears on the reactant side of any given equation. Always track sign changes carefully — reversing a reaction flips the sign of $\Delta H$.
